content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
September 9,
1993
Kathryn E.
Donovan
Pillsbury, Madison
& Sutro
Suite 1700,
400 Capitol Mall
Sacramento,
CA
95814
Re:
Your Requst for Advice
Our File No.
A—93—33l
Dear Ms.
Donovan:
This is in reply to your request for advice concerning
whether a proposed advertisement to be placed in newspapers by
your client, the Committee on JOBS
(“JOBS”),
is a reportable
contribution, expenditure or independent expenditure under the
Political Reform Act
(the “Act”)
.
Your letter seeks confirmation of the advice given to you by
me and Carla Wardlow,
the Chief of the Commission’s Technical
Assistance Division,
on August 25,
1993.
QUESTION
Would the attached advertisement,
if printed in a newspaper,
result in JOBS making a reportable contribution,
expenditure or
independent expenditure under the Act?
CONCLUSION
The advertisement would not result in JOBS making a
reportable contribution,
expenditure or independent expenditure
under the Act.
FACTS
JOBS is a non—profit corporation comprised of major San
Francisco Bay Area corporations established to promote a healthy
business climate in the City of San Francisco.
JOBS does not
sponsor or make contributions to any political committees.
1
Govermnent Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Goverrunent Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807
•
Sacramento, CA
95812
•
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—93—331
Page
2
A municipal election in the City of San Francisco is
scheduled for November 2,
1993.
The voters will vote on the
offices of City Attorney and City Treasurer.
In addition,
28
local ballot measures will appear on the ballot, several of which
would amend the City Charter.
Before any charter reform measures were proposed for the
November ballot,
JOBS conducted an extensive study of the City
Charter and concluded that charter reform was necessary to improve
the .business climate in the City.
In order to ensure that the
City’s voters are aware of the importance of the charter reform
issues,
JOBS has prepared the attached newspaper advertisement.
JOBS plans to run the advertisement in certain neighborhood
newspapers in the City between now and the November election.
None of the San Francisco Supervisors mentioned in the
advertisement will be on the November ballot.
Also,
JOBS does not
plan to act,
in running the advertisement,
“at the behest” of any
candidates or committees.
ANALYSIS
Under the Act,
an organization becomes a “committee” which
generally must report its expenditures if,
among other things, it
makes independent expenditures totalling $1,000 or more in a
calendar year.
(Section 82013 (b) (c).)
Furthermore, if the
organization makes an expenditure of
$100 or more at the behest of
a candidate,
the candidate must report that expenditure as
a
contribution from the organization.
(Sections 82015,
84200-
84221.)
Under the facts you present,
JOBS will be spending money to
print this advertisement in newspapers, but will not be doing so
at the behest of any candidates.
Accordingly,
JOBS cannot,
by
definition,
be making a contribution.
The remaining issue is whether JOBS,
in spending funds to
print this advertisement,
is making an independent expenditure by
referring to the three San Francisco Supervisors or specific
ballot measures in the advertisement.
Section 82031 defines independent expenditure as follows:
“Independent
expenditure”
means
an
expenditure
made
by
any
person
in
connection
with
a
communication
which
expressly
advocates
the
election
or
defeat of a clearly identified
candidate
or
the
qualification,
passage or defeat
of a clearly identified measure,
or taken
as
a whole and in context,
unambiguously
urges a particular result in an
election
but which is not made to or at the behest
of the affected candidate or committee.


File No.
A—93--331
Page
3
Furthermore, Regulation 18225(b) (2) states that a
communication “expressly advocates” the nomination, election or
defeat of a candidate or the qualification,
passage or defeat of a
measure if it:
contains
express
words
of
advocacy
such
as
“vote for,” “elect,” “support,”
“cast
your
ballot,”
“vote
against,”
“defeat,”
“reject,” “sign petitions for”
or
otherwise
refers
to
a
clearly
identified
candidate
or measure so that
the
comiuimication,
taken
as
a
whole,
unaiDbiguously
urges
a particular result
in an election.
JOBS’ proposed advertisement ólearly identifies specific
candidates and measures and makes reference to the election at
which the voters will consider these measures.
However, taken as
a whole,
we do not feel that the advertisement “unambiguously
urges a particular result in an election.”
The advertisement does
not urge the voters to vote in a particular way,
nor does it
unambiguously call for a particular result.
Though one could
possibly conclude that the advertisement indeed supports the
charter reforms proposed by these measures,
we cannot say that it
“unambiguously” does so.
It is also reasonable to read the
advertisement in a purely informational context.
Accordingly,
we
conclude that the advertisement does not expressly advocate the
election or defeat of any candidate or the passage or defeat of
any measure and that JOBS’ expenditure, therefore,
is not an
independent expenditure under the Act.
Should you have additional questions, please contact me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely.,
Wayne Ordos
Execut
e Dire tor
I Scott Hallabrin
Assistant General Counsel
SH:ak

independent expenditure under the Act?

reportable contribution,
expenditure or independent expenditure
under the Act.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Would the attached advertisement,
if printed in a newspaper,
result in JOBS making a reportable contribution,
expenditure or
independent expenditure under the Act?

CONCLUSION: The advertisement would not result in JOBS making a
reportable contribution,
expenditure or independent expenditure
under the Act.
